feed.title: [BIP Draft] Segregated Data: a prunable, script-isolated block region for data carriage

entry.title: [BIP Draft] Segregated Data: a prunable, script-isolated block region for data carriage
entry.summary:
The challenges of maintaining blockchain consensus following a soft fork are multifaceted and complex. When a segment of the network decides against adopting new rules introduced by a soft fork, opting instead to continue with older versions or disabling new rules, the potential for significant blockchain reorganizations becomes evident. This scenario unfolds when miners, perhaps indifferent to rule changes, continue to mine blocks that fail to comply with the new regulations. Such blocks, while possibly accumulating greater proof-of-work, would be rejected by nodes enforcing the new rules, which persist in recognizing only the compliant chain as valid.
This divergence in chain recognition can cause enforcing nodes to remain on a less work-intensive but rule-compliant chain. As non-compliant blocks are mined, they may continue to form a parallel chain which, due to its higher work proof, could later become relevant again once the retention window for the new rules expires. For instance, if there is a 100-block retention policy, beyond this period, blocks previously considered invalid due to missing data would then be recognized as valid. Consequently, enforcing nodes would need to undertake substantial blockchain reorganizations to align with the now-longer, compliant chain, resulting in potentially large reversals of transactions and loss of mining rewards for those on the initially shorter chain.
Furthermore, such scenarios underscore the inherent tension between node enforcement and miner compliance. While node enforcement aims to uphold network rules regardless of miner actions, the principle of "eventual consensus" in Bitcoin suggests that the most work-dedicated chain will ultimately prevail, assuming it eventually adheres to network rules. This dynamic suggests that unless a majority of the hashrate supports the enforcement of new rules, the stability and reliability of soft forks are questionable. Miners might disregard retention rules if they believe that non-compliance won't lead to enduring consequences, thus undermining the soft fork's effectiveness.
In essence, the effectiveness of soft forks in blockchain networks heavily relies on the cooperative interaction between miner behavior and node rule enforcement. Without sufficient hashrate support for the newly implemented rules, the network risks experiencing significant disruptions, including frequent and extensive reorganizations that could compromise transaction reliability and network integrity. Thus, the balance between maintaining rule integrity through node enforcement and accommodating miner incentives remains a critical consideration in the governance of blockchain protocols.
